message.content:
Test file: src/game-of-life.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/game-of-life.ts
Passing tests: 9
Recent Green phase: No change — block (still life) test already passed.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by measuring the current state of the implementation.

content.text:
All 9 tests pass and ESLint reports zero violations. Now let me compute PRE measurements.

## Step 0: Pre-Measurement (Smells + Cognitive Complexity)

- **ESLint smells: 0**
- **Cognitive complexity violations: 0**

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation

Function inventory:
- `nextGeneration(cells)` — clearly reveals intent: computes next state
- `cellKey([x,y])` — clearly reveals intent: encodes a cell to a string
- `neighborsOf([x,y])` — clearly reveals intent: lists 8 neighbors
- `candidateCells(liveCells)` — clearly reveals intent: cells to check (live + their neighbors)
- Inner closures: `isAlive`, `countLiveNeighbors`, `survivesOrIsBorn` — all read well

Constants `SURVIVAL_MIN_NEIGHBORS`, `SURVIVAL_MAX_NEIGHBORS`, `SURVIVAL_NEIGHBOR_COUNTS`, `REPRODUCTION_NEIGHBORS` are clear.

Naming is solid.

## Step 2: Pre-Measurement (APP Mass)

**`nextGeneration` (the main function):**
- Constants: 0
- Bindings: `cells`, `liveCells`, `isAlive`, `cell` (×3 in arrows), `countLiveNeighbors`, `survivesOrIsBorn`, `liveNeighbors` = ~9
- Invocations: `new Set`, `cells.map(cellKey)`, `liveCells.has`, `cellKey`, `neighborsOf`, `.filter`, `.length`, `countLiveNeighbors`, `isAlive`, `SURVIVAL_NEIGHBOR_COUNTS.includes`, `candidateCells`, `.filter` = ~12
- Conditionals: 1 (ternary)
- Loops: 0 (map/filter counted as invocations here, but let's be consistent — they are iteration)
- Assignments: 0

Let me redo more carefully treating .map/.filter as loops:
- Constants: 0
- Bindings: 9
- Invocations: ~9 (excluding map/filter as loops)
- Conditionals: 1
- Loops: 3 (cells.map, .filter, .filter)
- Assignments: 0

Mass: 0 + 9 + 18 + 4 + 15 + 0 = **46**

**`cellKey`:** bindings 2, invocations 0, mass = 2

**`neighborsOf`:** bindings 4 (x, y, dx, dy), invocations 1 (map could be loop), loops 1, mass = 4 + 5 = 9

**`candidateCells`:**
- Bindings: `liveCells`, `candidates`, `cell`, `neighbor` = 4
- Invocations: `new Map`, `cellKey`(×2), `candidates.set`(×2), `neighborsOf`, `[...candidates.values()]` = ~7
- Loops: 2 (for of, for of)
- Assignments: 2 (candidates.set is technically invocation — but they are mutations of the map)

Treating `candidates.set` as assignments (Map mutation): 2 assignments + 5 invocations.
Mass: 4 + 10 + 10 + 12 = **36**

## Step 3: Pre-Measurement (McCabe)

- `nextGeneration`: start 1, ternary +1 = **2**
- `cellKey`: 1
- `neighborsOf`: 1
- `candidateCells`: start 1, for-of +1, for-of +1 = **3**

## Step 4: Apply ONE Refactoring

The code is already quite clean. Let me look for one targeted improvement.

The function `nextGeneration` has the highest APP mass (~46). Looking at it: the closures `isAlive`, `countLiveNeighbors`, `survivesOrIsBorn` each capture variables and live inside. The ternary inside `survivesOrIsBorn` is the only branch.

One observation: the rule "alive with 2-3 neighbors OR dead with exactly 3 neighbors" can be unified. The pattern is:
- alive + 2 neighbors → survives
- alive + 3 neighbors → survives  
- dead + 3 neighbors → born

Which simplifies to: **3 live neighbors always**, OR **alive with 2 live neighbors**.

This unifies the rules and removes the ternary. Let me try.

content.content: The file /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_17-11-43_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text:
Wait — that constant name is awful (`SURVIVAL_WITH_BIRTH_NEIGHBORS_OR_TWO`). Rule 2 says clarity trumps mass. Let me reconsider — the original ternary read more clearly:

> "If alive: survives with 2 or 3. If dead: born with exactly 3."

That's the literal rule. Unifying it as "3 always, OR 2+alive" is clever but obscures intent. Let me revert this change because it violates Rule 2 (intent).

content.content: The file /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_17-11-43_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text:
Task tracking isn't needed for this focused refactoring pass. Let me try a different angle — looking at `candidateCells`, which has McCabe 3 and uses a Map purely as a deduplicated set.

The pattern there is: collect (key → cell) pairs, then take values. We could simplify by collecting candidates into a set-like structure. Actually the Map is necessary because we need to dedupe by key while preserving the actual `Cell` value. So that's reasonable.

Another angle: the inner closures in `nextGeneration` all close over `liveCells`. They're defined inline which makes the function long. But extracting would require passing `liveCells` to each — that adds invocation cost.

Let me try a different angle: there's a subtle inefficiency — `cellKey` is called many times for the same neighbor (in `candidateCells` and again in `isAlive`). But that's a performance concern, not a simple-design concern.

Looking carefully, I think the most defensible change is: the `survivesOrIsBorn` arrow function with its block body and intermediate variable could be a simple expression, AND the closures aren't actually needed as locally-scoped — they could be top-level helpers parameterized on `liveCells`. But pulling them out adds mass without clear benefit.

Let me look one more time at what could shrink. The naming of `survivesOrIsBorn` and the use of `SURVIVAL_NEIGHBOR_COUNTS.includes()` for a 2-element array is interesting. Using `.includes()` on a 2-element static array is more invocation cost than a comparison. Let me try:

content.text:
All 9 tests still green. ESLint still 0 violations.

## Step 6: Post-Measurement (APP + McCabe)

Let me recompute APP mass for `nextGeneration` after the change:

**Pre `nextGeneration`:**
- Removed: `SURVIVAL_NEIGHBOR_COUNTS.includes(liveNeighbors)` — 1 invocation (.includes)
- Added: `liveNeighbors >= MIN && liveNeighbors <= MAX` — 2 bindings refs + `&&` (which is a conditional/branch in McCabe terms, but in APP we'd treat the comparison operators as invocations roughly)

Actually let me be more careful. The `&&` adds a McCabe branch. So:

**McCabe for `nextGeneration`:**
- PRE: start 1 + ternary +1 = **2**
- POST: start 1 + ternary +1 + `&&` +1 = **3**

McCabe went UP. That's a worse measurement. Let me revert and reconsider.

content.content: The file /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_17-11-43_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text:
Reverted. Let me try yet another angle. Looking at `candidateCells` (McCabe 3, mass ~36) — this has the highest McCabe. It uses a `Map` for deduplication. A cleaner approach: use a `Set<string>` of keys and a separate `Map<string, Cell>` is redundant — actually no, we need the actual cell coordinates back. 

Wait, looking again: the candidate cells are all integer coordinates, so we could rebuild a Cell from a key string. But that's parsing — adds invocations.

Alternative: collect all candidate keys, then return them parsed back. That doesn't help mass.

A cleaner reading might be: `candidateCells` could be expressed as a `flatMap` + dedup:

content.text:
All tests still pass, 0 ESLint violations.

## Step 6: Post-Measurements for `candidateCells` + new `deduplicateCells`

**McCabe:**
- `candidateCells` PRE: 1 + for-of + nested for-of = **3**
- `candidateCells` POST: 1 (no branches; just flatMap) = **1**
- `deduplicateCells` POST: 1 (no branches; map then spread) = **1**

McCabe combined PRE: 3. POST: 1 + 1 = 2 combined (but each individual function went down — primary target `candidateCells` went 3 → 1).

**APP Mass for `candidateCells`:**
- PRE: 
  - Bindings: `liveCells`, `candidates`, `cell`, `neighbor` = 4
  - Invocations: `new Map`, `cellKey`(×2), `candidates.set`(×2), `neighborsOf`, `[...candidates.values()]`, `.values()` = ~7
  - Loops: 2 (for-of, for-of)
  - Mass: 4 + 14 + 10 = **28**
- POST:
  - Bindings: `liveCells`, `allCells`, `cell` = 3
  - Invocations: `flatMap`, `neighborsOf`, `deduplicateCells` = 3 (treating flatMap as loop)
  - Loops: 1 (flatMap)
  - Mass: 3 + 6 + 5 = **14**

**APP Mass for `deduplicateCells` (new):**
- Bindings: `cells`, `byKey`, `cell` = 3
- Invocations: `cells.map`, `cellKey`, `new Map`, `[...byKey.values()]`, `.values()` = ~4
- Loops: 1 (map)
- Mass: 3 + 8 + 5 = **16**

Combined POST mass: 14 + 16 = 30. **PRE was 28, POST is 30**. Slightly worse on combined mass, but McCabe dropped meaningfully (3 → 1 in target), and the new structure separates "collect candidates" from "deduplicate" — each function does one thing.

This is a tradeoff: +2 mass for −2 McCabe in target function + better separation of concerns (Rule 2: intent clearer). The names `candidateCells` and `deduplicateCells` each reveal exactly one operation.

Final verification.

content.text:
All 9 tests still pass, ESLint clean. Refactoring complete.

---

## Refactoring Complete

**Refactoring applied**: Split `candidateCells` into two functions — `candidateCells` (collect via `flatMap`) and `deduplicateCells` (dedup via `Map`). Replaces the nested `for-of` loops in the original.

**Also attempted and reverted**:
1. Unifying the survival/birth rules into `liveNeighbors === 3 || (liveNeighbors === 2 && isAlive)` — reverted because the new constant names would obscure intent (Rule 2 trumps APP mass).
2. Replacing `SURVIVAL_NEIGHBOR_COUNTS.includes(liveNeighbors)` with `>= MIN && <= MAX` — reverted because McCabe for `nextGeneration` went up from 2 to 3 due to the added `&&`.

**Pre/Post deltas:**

| Metric | PRE | POST | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| ESLint smells | 0 | 0 | unchanged (already clean) |
| Cognitive complexity violations | 0 | 0 | unchanged |
| McCabe `candidateCells` | 3 | 1 | -2 |
| McCabe `deduplicateCells` (new) | n/a | 1 | new |
| McCabe `nextGeneration` | 2 | 2 | unchanged |
| APP mass `candidateCells` | ~28 | ~14 | -14 |
| APP mass `deduplicateCells` (new) | n/a | ~16 | +16 |
| Combined mass (candidate+dedup) | 28 | 30 | +2 |
| Tests | 9/9 | 9/9 | green |

**Benefits:**
- `candidateCells` now reads as one operation: "live cells plus their neighbors, deduplicated"
- McCabe dropped from 3 to 1 in the worst-McCabe function (no more nested loops)
- The two responsibilities (gather, dedup) are now separately testable and named
- Small mass tradeoff (+2) accepted for the McCabe and intent gains

**Files changed:**
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_17-11-43_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts`
